provenance.title: Multidimensional stochastic liquidity in Kyle's model of informed trading
affiliations: Department of Mathematics, University of Michigan
Department of Operations Research and Financial Engineering, Princeton University
provenance.abstract: We develop a variational formulation of Kyle's model of informed trading that accommodates stochastic liquidity and multiple traded assets. The main equilibrium result is stated first: under a martingale dual condition, a matrix-valued martingale depth process generates a linear-Gaussian equil
key_claims: Recasts multi-asset Kyle-Back equilibrium as a primal-dual variational problem over decreasing posterior-covariance paths, inspired by causal optimal transport.

Identifies informed trading as optimal liquidation of an information inventory, with a matrix-valued martingale M* (market depth) as the dual/shadow-price variable and Lambda* = (M*)^{-1} as price impact.

States the equilibrium as conditional on a 'martingale dual condition' (MDC): the dual variational optimizer, which exists unconditionally only as an F^W-local martingale, must in fact be a true, interior martingale.

Verifies MDC and recovers known closed-form equilibria in the scalar case (Kyle 1985, Back 1992, Back-Pedersen 1998, Collin-Dufresne-Fos 2016, Ekren-Mostowski-Zitkovic 2025) and in the multidimensional common-eigenbasis case (Back-Cocquemas-Ekren-Lioui 2020); the fully general matrix case is reduced to an open, fully-coupled matrix FBSDE.
Establishes an independently-interesting Doob-Meyer decomposition theorem for general (not necessarily symmetric) matrix-valued local submartingales.

architecture.primary_variant: General matrix-valued variational Kyle-Back equilibrium (Theorem 3.3, conditional on MDC)

modules.name: PrimalCovarianceLiquidationSolver
modules.operation_type: stochastic_optimal_control (calculus of variations over decreasing matrix-valued covariance paths)
modules.input_shape: Sigma_0 = C in S^n_++ (n x n SPD prior covariance), sigma_t in S^n_++ (n x n noise-volatility process)
modules.output_shape: Sigma_dot_t in S^n_- (n x n path, a.e. t in [0,T]); optimal Sigma*_t

parameters.objective: sup E[ int_0^T tr(sqrt(-sigma_t Sigma_dot_t sigma_t)) dt ]

modules.notes: This is the market-maker-side primal problem: state is posterior covariance of v-tilde, control is instantaneous information-release rate.

modules.name: DualMartingaleDepthSolver
modules.input_shape: C in S^n_++, sigma_t in S^n_++
modules.output_shape: M*_t in S^n_++, an F^W-martingale (market depth); Lambda*_t = (M*_t)^{-1} (price impact)

parameters.objective: inf_{M in M} [ (1/2) tr(C M_0) + (1/2) E[int_0^T tr(sigma_t^2 M_t^{-1}) dt] ]

modules.notes: Existence of a local-martingale optimizer is unconditional (Theorem 4.5, via a Komlos/Delbaen-Schachermayer compactness argument over n(n+1)/2 scalar coordinates plus a matrix Doob-Meyer decomposition). Whether this optimizer is a TRUE, interior martingale is the martingale dual condition (MDC, Definition 3.1) and is the paper's central open structural hypothesis in the general case.

modules.name: MatrixDoobMeyerDecomposition
modules.operation_type: theoretical_lemma (decomposition theorem, used inside DualMartingaleDepthSolver's existence proof)
modules.input_shape: X_t: n x n matrix-valued local submartingale
modules.output_shape: X_t = M_t + A_t, M local martingale, A nondecreasing predictable, A_0=0
modules.notes: Generalizes scalar Doob-Meyer to general (not necessarily symmetric) matrix submartingales via the symmetric part Z=(X+X^T)/2 and antisymmetric part Y=(X-X^T)/2 (a martingale), decomposing Z coordinatewise along all e_i+e_j directions.

modules.name: KalmanBucyPriceFilter
modules.output_shape: P*_t = E[v-tilde | F^M_t] = p_0 + xi*_t; posterior covariance Sigma*_t = Var(v-tilde | F^M_t)
modules.notes: Standard Kalman-Bucy filter with the static hidden state v-tilde; equilibrium selects Sigma_t = Riccati solution matching Sigma_dot* from the primal problem.

modules.name: InsiderOptimalStrategy
modules.notes: Inconspicuous (E[dX*_t/dt | F^M_t]=0) and drives xi*_T = v-tilde - p_0 a.s.

modules.name: MDCVerification_ScalarCase
modules.output_shape: closed-form M*_t, recovers Kyle 1985 / Back 1992 / Back-Pedersen 1998 / Collin-Dufresne-Fos 2016
modules.notes: Includes an explicit deterministic-growth case (5.4, CDF 2016) solved two ways (primal Euler-Lagrange under a measure change, and dual via a linear BSDE), and a general scalar stochastic-liquidity case (5.5) solved via Yong-Zhou's stochastic maximum principle, reducing to the quadratic BSDE of Ekren-Mostowski-Zitkovic (2025).

modules.name: MDCVerification_CommonEigenbasis
modules.input_shape: C and sigma_t simultaneously diagonalizable along a fixed orthogonal basis V for all t
modules.output_shape: M*_t block-diagonalizes into n independent scalar problems (Proposition 5.2), recovers Back-Cocquemas-Ekren-Lioui 2020 constant-volatility case
modules.notes: Reduces the multidimensional problem to n scalar problems solvable by MDCVerification_ScalarCase machinery.

modules.name: GeneralMatrixFBSDE_OpenProblem

parameters.backward_eq: dG~_t = M_t alpha~*_t G~_t alpha~*_t M_t dt + sigma_t^2 dt + U~_t dW_t

modules.notes: FLAGGED: this is explicitly an open problem in the paper (no known wellposedness theory for this class of fully coupled, quadratic-driver, singular-adjoint FBSDE). Any code reproduction can only implement this as a numerical experiment/heuristic solver, not a verified algorithm.

differences.method: closed form / linear BSDE / quadratic BSDE via stochastic maximum principle

variants.name: Common eigenbasis (n>1, C and sigma_t co-diagonalizable)

differences.solves_MDC: yes, reduces to n scalar problems (Section 5.6)
open problem, reduces to unsolved coupled matrix FBSDE (Section 5.7)

mathematical_spec.name: Equilibrium price process
mathematical_spec.latex: P^*_t = p_0 + \xi^*_t = p_0 + \int_0^t (M^*_s)^{-1}\, dY^*_s = E[\tilde v \mid \mathcal F^M_t]

mathematical_spec.name: Insider value function / upper bound (verification potential)
Matrix-valued Doob-Meyer decomposition
mathematical_spec.latex: X_t = M_t + A_t,\quad M\ \text{local martingale},\ A\ \text{nondecreasing predictable},\ A_0=0

mathematical_spec.name: Stochastic maximum principle Hamiltonian (scalar stochastic liquidity)
General matrix FBSDE (open problem)
mathematical_spec.latex: d\tilde G_t = M_t\tilde\alpha^*_t\tilde G_t\tilde\alpha^*_t M_t\,dt + \sigma_t^2\,dt + \tilde U_t\,dW_t,\quad dM_t = M_t\tilde\alpha^*_t M_t\,dW_t

metrics: Analytical/closed-form agreement with prior equilibria (exact recovery, not a numerical error metric)
special_conditions: This is a purely theoretical paper: there are no numerical experiments, datasets, or empirical benchmark tables. 'Evaluation' consists entirely of analytically verifying that the general variational construction specializes to exactly recover five known closed-form Kyle-Back equilibria under successively more restrictive assumptions on C and sigma_t (Section 5). No compute/hardware is reported since nothing is trained or simulated numerically in the paper itself.

implementation_assumptions.assumption: A faithful 'reproduction' of this paper is a symbolic/numerical verification suite (e.g. in a CAS or via Monte Carlo simulation of the SDEs/BSDEs), not a trained ML model — there is no dataset, network, or optimizer to reproduce in the conventional ArXivist sense.
implementation_assumptions.basis: inferred from paper content (pure stochastic-control/probability theory paper, MSC-classified 91G15/91G80/60G44/60H30/49N90)
alternatives: Treat only the 5 closed-form benchmark cases (Section 5.1-5.4, 5.6) as reproducible unit tests, since Section 5.7's general case is an explicitly open problem

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Numerical verification of the scalar stochastic-liquidity case (Section 5.5) requires a numerical BSDE solver (e.g. deep BSDE / Han-Jentzen-E method, or classical least-squares Monte Carlo) for the quadratic BSDE (5.44)-(5.45), since the paper only proves existence via citation to Ekren-Mostowski-Zitkovic (2025) without giving a numerical scheme.
implementation_assumptions.basis: common practice for quadratic BSDEs in the literature; not specified in this paper
alternatives: Restrict numerical verification to cases with closed-form M*_t (Sections 5.1-5.4, 5.6), which require no BSDE solver

implementation_assumptions.assumption: For any numerical experiment, time is discretized on a uniform grid on [0,T] with a Euler-Maruyama scheme for the driving Brownian motions B and W; the paper works in continuous time throughout and specifies no discretization.
implementation_assumptions.basis: standard numerical practice for SDE/BSDE simulation
alternatives: Exact simulation where closed-form transition densities are available (e.g. constant-sigma Kyle 1985 case)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The dimension n (number of traded assets) for any multi-asset numerical illustration should be small (e.g. n=2 or n=3) for tractable visualization/inspection of matrix-valued paths.
alternatives: n=1 (scalar, fully covered by paper's own benchmarks)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Matrix inversions of M_t/Sigma_t in any numerical scheme require a positive-definiteness safeguard (e.g. eigenvalue clipping or Cholesky-based solves) since the theory requires M_t, Sigma_t in S^n_++ but a naive discretization can produce numerically singular or indefinite matrices near t=T (where Sigma*_T=0).
implementation_assumptions.basis: standard numerical linear algebra practice for matrix Riccati/covariance paths
alternatives: Truncate the time horizon slightly before T to avoid the degenerate boundary

implementation_assumptions.assumption: There is no reference implementation, code repository, or supplementary numerical artifact accompanying this paper (none is mentioned in the text).
implementation_assumptions.basis: no code/data availability statement found in the extracted text

ambiguities.description: The fully general matrix-valued case (non-commuting C, sigma_t) reduces to a fully coupled matrix FBSDE with a quadratic and singular driver. The paper explicitly states wellposedness of this FBSDE is open and does not provide a numerical or existence-theoretic resolution. Any 'implementation' of this stage can only be a heuristic/exploratory numerical experiment, not a verified reproduction.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Flag this stage as unimplementable-with-guarantees; restrict code reproduction to Sections 5.1-5.4 and 5.6 (scalar and common-eigenbasis cases) where MDC is proved to hold.
alternatives: Attempt a deep-BSDE-style numerical solver for (5.50)-(5.51) purely as an exploratory heuristic, clearly labeled as unverified

ambiguities.description: MDC is an abstract 'interior minimizer' condition on a matrix martingale; the paper proves it holds in specific benchmark cases but gives no general checkable numerical criterion for arbitrary (C, sigma_t) input data.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: For arbitrary user-supplied (C, sigma_t) outside the benchmark cases, MDC verification cannot be automated; the code should only claim to verify equilibria for the cases explicitly covered in Section 5.
alternatives: Numerically approximate the compactness argument of Theorem 4.5 (Komlos-type convex combinations) and empirically test whether the resulting limit stays uniformly bounded away from the boundary of S^n_++, as an empirical (non-rigorous) proxy for MDC

ambiguities.description: Several derivations invoke a Novikov-type condition on the volatility-of-volatility process nu(t, sigma_t) to justify a change of measure (Girsanov); the paper assumes but does not verify this condition holds for a generic choice of nu, m.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: For numerical experiments, choose nu, m bounded and deterministic-in-sigma (e.g. constant or affine) so Novikov's condition trivially holds.
alternatives: Use a truncated/localized version of nu for well-posedness, at the cost of an approximation error
